note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's break tags and sentence boundaries. Block windows are the blocks' measured positions in the joined master: the Sigma(audio+0.4) formula predicts 220.710s but the ffprobed master is 220.290612s (0.42s off, past the +-0.3s gate), so each block start is the measured onset from FFT cross-correlation of each sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono PCM; every block correlated at 1.000). Windows are contiguous (each block endS = the next block's measured onset; the joined gaps are ~0.363s, not 0.4s). Final end = ffprobed master duration 220.290612s.

phrases.text: Every configuration screen in Wash Central lives behind one gear.
Click it.
Settings opens as its own full-screen area, with its own sidebar and its own front door.
Setup tickets all end up here sooner or later,
so this walk is about learning the floor plan before you need it.
Check the counter, top right.
Sixteen categories, ninety two pages.
That's the platform's entire configuration surface in one line.
Nobody memorizes ninety two pages.
You memorize how they're grouped, and the groups do the remembering for you.
Below the heading sits a search box.
When you already know the page name, type it here and jump straight in.
Much faster than scanning cards.
We'll leave it empty today and learn the layout the slow way, once.
Now sweep the card grid.
Sixteen cards, one per category, each with a one line summary of what it holds.
Locations, payments, hardware, the customer portal, the A-I agent.
This lesson stays inside the first card, General,
because that's where a client's account and org structure are defined.
Open General.
The sidebar expands to nine pages, and the first of them, Account Information, opens on its own.
Read the names as they appear: profit centers, departments, categories.
Those three are the spine we came for.
Start at the top of Account Information.
Fifty six locations across fifteen states,
running in multi operation mode on an active license.
This page is the client's site registry.
When you need to confirm what a client actually runs, and where, this is the record of truth.
Scan the list below.
Each row is one site with its company, its operation mode, and its contact details.
There's a New Record button at the top right.
Locate it, then leave it alone.
Adding a site is production admin work, never part of a training walk.
Next page: Profit Centers.
Four records.
Wash, lube, detail, and store.
These are the top level revenue buckets,
and every sale in the platform rolls up into exactly one of them.
Four short rows that shape every financial report the client will ever read.
Now Departments.
The same four names again, and that's the point.
Departments map the operational side of the business onto those same revenue buckets,
so labor and sales line up when a report compares them.
All four active, all maintained by Micro Logic.
Then Categories, where the detail lives.
The tiles say forty nine.
The list header says sixty six records.
They disagree, and the list wins; trust the table count, not the tile.
Each category refines one profit center,
which is how a single wash bucket splits into dozens of reportable lines.
One more page, and it comes with a trick.
Dashboard Settings is filed under Analytics, not General, whatever older material tells you.
The platform wins.
Ten dashboards, one per user level,
deciding what an administrator, a manager, or a cashier sees the moment they sign in.
Click Close Settings.
You're back in the app, exactly where you left it.
Five pages read, nothing touched.
So that's the General group.
The Settings home reaches ninety two pages, and search jumps you to any of them.
Account Information holds the sites.
Profit centers, departments, and categories classify every sale, in that order.
And Dashboard Settings lives under Analytics,
which will save you a hunt the first time someone asks.
